name: South Dakota Searchlight
title: South Dakota inches toward studying nuclear power as data center energy demands loom
description: South Dakota Republican Gov. Larry Rhoden’s proposed $300,000 nuclear energy study is a step toward the inevitable, energy and economic development professionals say. “We’re going to ultimately have to get to nuclear,” said CEO and President of NorthWestern Energy Brian Bird during a panel discussion in Sioux Falls in October. Emerging industries including d[...]
text:
The shuttered Three Mile Island nuclear power plant near Middletown, Pennsylvania, pictured on Oct. 10, 2024. (Photo by Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images)South Dakota Republican Gov. Larry Rhoden’s proposed $300,000 nuclear energy study is a step toward the inevitable, energy and economic development professionals say.
“We’re going to ultimately have to get to nuclear,” said CEO and President of NorthWestern Energy Brian Bird during a panel discussion in Sioux Falls in October.
Emerging industries including data centers, Bird said, will “need a tremendous amount of energy to do what they need to do.”



A South Dakota Searchlight series examining the impact, barriers and concerns regarding efforts to build data centers in the state. Read more >


It takes two coal or natural gas plants, or three to four renewable resource-based plants, such as wind, water or renewable natural gas, to generate the same amount of electricity as one typical nuclear reactor, according to a 2023 South Dakota Legislative Research Council memo. 
Rhoden is seeking funding for the study in the next state budget. He expressed interest in nuclear energy — especially in the cheaper, small, modular reactors gaining interest across the nation — during the Tri-State Governors Conference in North Sioux City last summer, when he referenced heightened needs for power in the age of data centers and artificial intelligence.
“I think the time has come to revisit nuclear power, given the advancements in technology that have been made,” Rhoden said.
Rooms or buildings full of computer servers have been storing cellphone pictures, emails and social media accounts for years. What’s new are 100- to 1,000-acre warehouses full of servers for cryptocurrency and artificial intelligence. Those massive data centers with 30- to 1,000-megawatt loads have energy consumption equivalent to 29,000 to 800,000 residential customers.
South Dakota’s biggest data center consumes 30 megawatts, and the state has none of the vastly larger data centers that have proliferated elsewhere. Some of South Dakota’s elected officials question whether the state should incentivize the industry as many other states have, due in part to the massive energy demands of large data centers and the potential impacts on the availability and cost of electricity for other customers.
From left, Iowa’s Kim Reynolds, Nebraska’s Jim Pillen and South Dakota’s Larry Rhoden participate in the Tri-State Governors’ Conference in Dakota Dunes, South Dakota, on Aug. 26, 2025. (Photo by Makenzie Huber/South Dakota Searchlight)
Data center energy demands
Data centers make up 4.4% of annual U.S. electricity consumption, a figure that could triple by the end of the decade, according to a U.S. Congressional report.
South Dakota Public Utilities Commissioner Chris Nelson said a new power plant won’t have to be built to service every data center. The commission regulates investor-owned utility companies across the state.
Nelson said gigawatts of available electricity exist across the U.S. grid most of the time, which could be used to power data centers. The problem comes during extreme weather or peak hours, when supply gets tight. 
To prevent rate and reliability impacts, data centers are increasingly required to be “interruptible customers,” shutting down or switching to backup power during peak hours, Nelson explained. 

Public Utilities Commissioner Chris Nelson participates in a hearing July 25, 2023, in Fort Pierre on a permit application for the proposed Heartland Greenway carbon dioxide pipeline. (Photo by Joshua Haiar/South Dakota Searchlight)

“Any data center that’s going to be built is going to have a complete backup generator on site,” Nelson said, which would likely be diesel or natural gas.
Governor’s Office of Economic Development Commissioner Bill Even said if South Dakota pursues data centers and other industries requiring more electricity — which includes value-added agriculture, such as a soybean processing plant that opened recently in Mitchell — then South Dakota will need to invest in renewable energy like wind or improved hydroelectric generation in the short term and nuclear in the long term. Utilities will likely purchase power from other areas of the United States in the meantime. North Dakota, for example, produces a significant amount of natural gas.
“If you’re trying to get online quickly and want that base load power, you’re probably going to be looking at natural gas,” Even said.
NorthWestern considering sites for nuclear project
Data center critics worry ushering the industry into South Dakota will increase electricity rates as utilities build more power plants and pass the costs on to customers.
Small, modular nuclear reactors could offer scalable, localized power for high-load users like data centers, minimizing costs and risk to the grid, Bird said. Ideally, he added, the reactors could be placed next to high-load users.
“They can pay the upfront cost of the nuclear power, and the consumers would be much less harmed,” Bird said.

Brian Bird, president and CEO of Northwestern Energy, speaks on a panel at the SDSU Land and Lending Conference in Sioux Falls on Oct. 1, 2025. (Photo by Makenzie Huber/South Dakota Searchlight)

The company is evaluating potential vendors and project sites for a small, modular reactor, aiming for a 2030 construction date. NorthWestern would also explore federal grants and financing to “keep customer rates affordable,” a spokesperson with the company said. If other on-demand energy resources are retired, such as coal, a nuclear reactor “could be a cost-effective alternative.”
Rhoden’s proposed study will look at the regulatory hurdles in pursuing nuclear energy, environmental concerns, costs, types of equipment or infrastructure needed, and other barriers at the state or local level.
South Dakota does not produce any nuclear energy, but 4% of electricity consumed in the state comes from nuclear power produced elsewhere.
South Dakota is one of 11 states that has not signed an agreement with the federal Nuclear Regulatory Commission to take over authority of radioactive materials, including issuing licenses, conducting inspections and enforcing safety regulations over the industrial, medical and academic uses of radioactive material.
If lawmakers approve the study, Lt. Gov. Tony Venhuizen said a “reasonable timeframe” would be to hire a consultant by the end of spring 2026 with results reported by the end of 2026.
“This is a topic that once you move past studying and into implementing, it becomes more controversial,” Venhuizen said. “You have to look at the recommendations and ask if it’s something to move forward with.”
Addressing supply chain and nuclear waste 
The United States is the largest producer of nuclear energy in the world, though China is rapidly expanding and expected to surpass the U.S. 
Uranium is mined, processed and enriched into nuclear fuel. There were three uranium mines operating in the U.S. in 2021, sourcing a fraction of the country’s need. That’s up to 10 today. 
About 20% of electricity in the United States comes from nuclear energy, and nuclear power plant operators in the country source 99.8% of their uranium from other countries, including Canada, Kazakhstan and Russia, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.
Trump administration selects Black Hills uranium mining proposal for fast-track permitting program

President Donald Trump issued executive orders regarding uranium last year to streamline permitting, expedite environmental reviews and impose tariffs on imports that compete with American products.
EnCore Energy is one of the companies hoping to mine for uranium on the southern edge of South Dakota’s Black Hills. Janet Lee-Sheriff, head of communications for enCore, said the country has a “natural interest” in being self-reliant with energy.
“What is happening in your neighborhood can contribute to a solution needed for the state and country,” Lee-Sheriff said.
After uranium is mined, processed, enriched and used in nuclear power generation, radioactive waste is produced.
Eric Meyer, executive director of Generation Atomic, told attendees at the Sioux Metro Growth Alliance’s annual Growth Summit that nuclear waste is safely stored in “some of the most robust containers known to man,” adding that the containers are tested by dropping missiles on them. 
Generation Atomic is a nuclear energy advocacy organization. Meyer’s presentation at the summit aimed to address concerns regarding nuclear energy, including environmental and economic impacts from such projects.

A fact sheet from the Nuclear Energy Institute shows existing nuclear reactors for electric generation across the United States. (Nuclear Energy Institute)

There are about 90,000 tons of nuclear waste stored at over 100 sites in 39 states. Nuclear waste is stored on the power plant’s property before being removed, dried and sealed in welded stainless steel canisters that are stored outdoors in concrete vaults on concrete pads, according to Gerald Frankel, a materials science and engineering professor at The Ohio State University. As of 2024, there were more than 315,000 bundles of spent nuclear fuel rods in the U.S., and more than 3,800 dry storage casks in concrete vaults above ground, located at current and former power plants across the country.
The United States has been working toward building a permanent disposal site for nuclear waste in Nevada since 1987, but the work has been delayed by political and legal challenges.
Bill Even, the head of the South Dakota Governor’s Office of Economic Development, said nuclear power is “arguably safer” than it used to be, and the state needs information about how it could help meet growing energy demands.
“Advancements in science and technology are marching forward at a fast pace,” Even said, “and let’s keep an open mind to what the future may hold around new technology.”

name: South Dakota Searchlight
title: Sully County, home to SD’s largest data center, would welcome more with ‘open arms,’ official says
description: Citizens crowded county commission meetings and city council rooms in recent months, expressing concerns about potential data centers near Sioux Falls and the small South Dakota town of Toronto.  Three years ago, residents of Sully County did the same when a 30 megawatt data center was proposed near the hydroelectric power generated by the Oahe […]
text:
A portion of a data center owned by Big Watt Digital north of Pierre, pictured on Dec. 23, 2025. (Photo by Meghan O'Brien/South Dakota Searchlight)Citizens crowded county commission meetings and city council rooms in recent months, expressing concerns about potential data centers near Sioux Falls and the small South Dakota town of Toronto. 
Three years ago, residents of Sully County did the same when a 30 megawatt data center was proposed near the hydroelectric power generated by the Oahe Dam on the Missouri River, Sully County Planning and Zoning Board Chair Austin Gross told state lawmakers last winter.
“The meetings we had with the data center on the agenda were some of the highest attended meetings publicly that we’ve had since I’ve been on the board,” Gross said.



A South Dakota Searchlight series examining the impact, barriers and concerns regarding efforts to build data centers in the state. Read more >


The Sully County facility is now the largest data center in South Dakota. It’s expected to expand to 300 megawatts in the coming years. 
Running near continuously, that would be the same amount of energy as nearly 209,000 South Dakota residential customers, based on an estimate from South Dakota Public Utilities Commission Utility Analyst Darren Kearney. For comparison, Sioux Falls, the state’s largest city, has 92,477 housing units, according to the city’s Planning and Zoning Department.
Rooms or buildings full of computer servers have been storing cellphone pictures, emails and social media accounts for years. What’s new are 100- to 1,000-acre warehouses full of servers for cryptocurrency and artificial intelligence. Those massive data centers with 30- to 1,000-megawatt loads consume the equivalent amount of energy as 29,000 to 800,000 South Dakota residential customers.
South Dakota has none of the vastly larger data centers that have proliferated elsewhere. Some of South Dakota’s elected officials question whether the state should incentivize the industry as many other states have, due in part to the massive energy demands of large data centers and the potential impacts on the availability and cost of electricity for other customers.
The Sully County facility is for cryptocurrency mining, which uses continuously running computers and other digital infrastructure to record transactions and solve complex numerical problems that “mine” new digital currency. The facility is located about 23 miles north of Pierre and is owned by Big Watt Digital, which is based in Pierre. Big Watt Digital employs less than 20 people. Gross said the company’s planned expansion in Sully County could lead to 100 full-time jobs and another 300 construction jobs.
That may be small to other communities, Gross said, but growth is the biggest challenge for the state’s fifth least-populated county.
SUBSCRIBE: GET THE MORNING HEADLINES DELIVERED TO YOUR INBOX
“The opportunity to combat these issues are ever-fleeting,” Gross said. “You almost never find something like this to help a small town and community like ours.”
The new jobs also contribute to “Main Street spending,” County Commissioner Caleb Shepherd told lawmakers last year.
“Any additional data facilities we can attract to South Dakota will be met with open arms in Sully County,” Shepherd said.
Substantial wind and hydroelectric power are accessible at the site, in addition to plenty of rural land for expansions. The new load from the data center, Oahe Electric CEO Jordan Lamb told South Dakota Searchlight, helps retire old energy generation assets more quickly than if the co-op only served homes, small businesses and agriculture operations.
That ultimately benefits ratepayers because the co-op can pay off its existing equipment more quickly, Lamb added. For example, Big Watt paid to have a substation adjacent to the operation rebuilt.
The data center has become a major revenue source for the local school district, providing around $850,000 a year in gross receipts taxes, Lamb added.
The new revenue effectively turns the data center into a source of property tax relief, said Agar-Blunt-Onida Superintendent Jeremy Chicoine. 
Jeremy Chicoine serves as the superintendent for the Agar-Blunt-Onida School District in central South Dakota. (Courtesy of the Agar-Blunt-Onida School District)
The district dropped its capital outlay request, which is a property tax fund for equipment and building upgrades, by $600,000 last year. That’s nearly 13% of total school taxes paid in the district in fiscal year 2024.
The school district plans to reduce its general fund mill levy later this year if gross receipts revenue from Oahe Electric and Big Watt Digital continue to bring in such large revenues.
As the geographically largest school district east of the Missouri River, Agar-Blunt-Onida qualifies for sparsity funding from the state, with less than 250 students enrolled in the district. The school district, Chicoine said, would sacrifice roughly $85,000 in sparsity funding – since sparse school districts have to set their general fund mill levy at their maximum rate. 
The reduction would provide continued property tax relief for residents while still meeting needs of the students, Chicoine said.
“We’re a fat school district for our enrollment size,” he added.
Agar-Blunt-Onida offers several programs normally not available at such small, rural schools because of the sizable gross receipts tax revenue, including career and technical education programs and music, physical education and art for all grade levels.
He added that the school district will start a public school preschool program with the extra funding next school year. The decision is in response to a local private preschool closing.
The school district is one of six in South Dakota that doesn’t rely on state funding, since its budget is covered entirely by local tax collections — property taxes, gross receipts and other taxes.
“We’re not out there to fleece tax dollars,” Chicoine said. “We’re trying to invest in our kids and community in a responsible fashion that doesn’t rake the taxpayer over the coals.”

name: Source New Mexico
title: NM Republican and Democrat team up to streamline disaster spending — and rein in governor
description: House Bill 180 seeks to give the state's system for funding disaster response its first major update in more than 70 years.
text:
New Mexico Rep. Harlan Vincent (R-Ruidoso Downs) on opening day of the Legislature, Jan. 20, 2026. He said he hopes House Bill 180 will modernize the state’s disaster response while also ensuring the executive branch can’t spend from the state’s savings accounts without legislative permission. (Patrick Lohmann/Source NM)Two New Mexico representatives from disaster-prone areas are co-sponsoring legislation this session to streamline the state’s response to emergencies, while also ensuring the governor does not bypass the Legislature when spending millions on disasters. 
Reps. Harlan Vincent (R-Ruidoso Downs) and Susan Herrera (D-Embudo) are sponsoring House Bill 180, an 18-page bill both legislators described in interviews with Source NM this week as comprehensive and necessary to modernize the state’s response to increasingly common and expensive disasters. 
A House committee approved the bill unanimously Friday morning.
The bill seeks to reform a system that has “changed little in the past 70 years, despite the changing nature of emergencies and the contemporary structure of state finances,” according to a Legislative Finance Committee analysis.
The bill would eliminate the current $750,000 cap state law places on every emergency order Gov. Michelle Lujan Grisham issues in response to a host of natural and man-made disasters. 
Especially since the COVID-19 pandemic, emergencies have been far more expensive, requiring the governor to issue hundreds of identical emergency orders to adequately mobilize resources necessary for wildfires, floods or other crises.
By the #s: NM’s beleaguered emergency fund source of growing tension between governor, Legislature

The cap has become a “nightmare” for state and local officials trying to account for spending during a natural disaster, said Vincent, whose Ruidoso-area district has experienced devastating fires and floods in recent years. 
The legislation also seeks to impose additional checks on how the executive branch funds disaster response. Lujan Grisham’s recent spending has caused friction with the Legislature, including members of her own party, who say she is spending without legislative approval. 
Instead of having one big pot to fund all types of disasters, the bill would create two separate funds, one for natural disasters like wildfires or floods and another to pay for “non-natural” disasters. The bill defines the latter category as events that threaten public health or safety but don’t result from natural forces or an enemy attack. 
Lawmakers will allocate money into each fund, Vincent said, but require the governor to seek the Legislature’s approval via a special legislative session if she wants to spend more than has been allocated. Vincent said that the amount in each fund will likely be $50 million, though he’s still working on an amendment to that effect. 
The bill “streamlines the emergency funding process, so leadership can give liberally while maintaining fiscal responsibility and managerial oversight,” Vincent said. A Republican, Vincent quickly clarified had used the word “liberally” to mean “generously.” 
Between July 2024 and November of last year, the Legislature allocated $150 million into the Appropriation Contingency Fund, from which the governor can spend at her discretion on disasters. 
But over that same period, the governor’s disaster spending reached roughly $380 million. She drew the extra funds from a state savings account called the Operating Reserve. Lawmakers have said that upsets the balance of powers and could be illegal.
The funding in that period went for wildfires, floods or a mix of both across the state, as well as National Guard deployments to Albuquerque and Española and the state’s provision of federal food assistance benefits during a government shutdown. 
Earlier this week, Sen. Craig Brandt (R-Rio Rancho) reiterated his view that that the governor’s continued spending out of the Operating Reserve is “inappropriate,” “unconstitutional” and “disrespectful to the branch” during an exchange at a committee meeting with Miguel Aguilar, adjutant general of the New Mexico National Guard and acting secretary for the state’s disaster response agency. 
Aguilar responded that, “I think we’d be glad to have that conversation, but as it currently exists, the governor will tell you that she has the authority to do what she’s done.”
New Mexico Legislature passes, acting governor signs $162.5M to cover SNAP payments if needed

The governor’s office did not immediately respond to Source’s Friday request for comment on the bill. Her team has previously defended the spending as appropriate in the face of mounting disaster costs that require speedy allocation of resources. 
Herrera, the Democratic co-sponsor, told Source she acknowledges the state has had a “terrible series of natural disasters.” 
“I get that, but we need to set limits. We need to increase the amount of money that she gets for natural disasters, and then we need to stop it,” she said. “And then she needs to come back for legislative appropriation authority, because the appropriators are the ones who do the budget.”
While the bill was written with the current governor’s spending in mind, its major provisions won’t go into effect until after she leaves office, according to the bill text.
Harlan told Source that he was deeply appreciative of the governor’s help during the Ruidoso area since fires and floods hit the area beginning in summer 2024. That’s the reason he wanted the bill not to take effect until the next governor steps in.
“The governor has been more than fair with my community, and I don’t want to take a poke at her like that,” he said. 
The bill heads next to the House Appropriations and Finance Committee.

name: Mississippi Today
title: Where there’s ice there’s fire? Jacksonians battle the familiar blaze amid winter freeze
description: Khristina Miracle pitches what she can salvage of her burned belongings down to a shopping cart at the Chapel Ridge Apartments, located at 1636 Raymond Road in south Jackson, Monday, Jan. 26, 2026.
text:
The audio version of this story is AI generated and is not human reviewed. It may contain errors or inaccuracies.
As the sun set in Jackson and the temperature registered a frigid 25 degrees, a plume of black and white smoke brought onlookers to the sidewalk at the Village Apartments on Raymond Road. 
“It’s too cold for this,” remarked a woman filming the Monday blaze consuming unit 31A. “Check on y’all people.” 
Though the capital city was spared from the devastating conditions that hit north Mississippi last weekend, Winter Storm Fern coincided with what’s known as “fire season” in Jackson.
A Jackson firefighter extinguishes a blaze that destroyed a home in the 6000 block of Floral Drive in north Jackson, Tuesday, Jan. 27, 2026. The cause of the fire is under investigation. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
All across the metro, structures burned: The abandoned Virginia College in northeast Jackson, a dilapidated brick house in northwest Jackson, a shed near a west Jackson interstate exchange, and an abandoned apartment complex in south Jackson, two and a half miles down the road from the Village. 
Conventional wisdom is that in Jackson, where poverty is high and freezes are rare, people will more frequently resort to unsafe means to stay warm in colder weather. This includes those who lack permanent shelter and may set a fire in an abandoned building. 
Abandoned home destroyed by a recent fire, located at the corner of Lamar and Cohea Streets north of downtown Jackson, Monday, Jan. 26, 2026. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
“People trying to stay warm, that’s what it is,” said Cleotha Sanders, the Jackson Fire Department’s chief of community relations and fire safety. 
But the number of structure fires this week roughly matched the annual daily average in previous years, an analysis showed. And, the nature of fire is unpredictable. During the recent winter storm, most of the fires were caused by electrical problems or by accident, according to JFD. The reasons – an over-used surge protector, even copper theft – were not related to a human trying to stay warm. 
Just four of the 10 structure fires that broke out across the city from Saturday to Tuesday occurred at dilapidated or abandoned structures known to be occupied by homeless people — and even then, the exact cause is unknown, said Charles Felton, JFD’s chief of investigations.
With a slim staff of three fire investigators – the fire department’s detectives – Felton said he prioritizes sending his officers to incidents that are likely arson. 
Other fires defied a simple explanation. Take, for instance, the fire that consumed a stucco home on Floral Drive in northwest Jackson on Tuesday. 
Jackson firefighters extinguish a fire that destroyed a home in the 6000 block of Floral Drive in north Jackson, Tuesday, Jan. 27, 2026. The cause of the fire is under investigation. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
The occupant, Luther Martin, stood with his hands in his leather bomber jacket as he watched nearly a dozen firefighters pour water on the former monastery where he has lived with his wife and children for six years. 
A Jackson firefighter extinguishes hotspots during after a blaze destroyed a home in the 6000 block of Floral Drive in north Jackson, Tuesday, Jan. 27, 2026. The cause of the fire is under investigation. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
Biscuit, his brown-and-white dog who stays outside, ambled over to Martin. But his cat, Shamrock, perished in the flames. 
Martin was at a business meeting at Broad Street when a neighbor called to notify him of the blaze. Instantly, his mind went to the homeless encampment that Martin said sits in the 65-acre woods behind his house. An intentional act, he thought. Through his nonprofit, the Luther Martin Evangelistic Association, Martin said he contracts with the state to run transitional homes in Jackson and the Delta for people leaving prison – a job that sometimes requires kicking people out for drug use or fighting. 
“So I got a lot of enemies,” he said. 
About a month ago, Martin said the tool shop behind his house burned down. JFD could not determine the source of that fire but still, Martin said, “that’s just a heck of a coincidence.” 
The self-described global evangelist estimates he lost $80,000 worth of furniture and family heirlooms in the fire, but he didn’t have insurance. The property was given to him, he said, through a “lifetime lease” from an Oxford-based foundation called Adventus that owns the home. 
Jackson firefighters extinguish a fire that destroyed a home in the 6000 block of Floral Drive in north Jackson, Tuesday, Jan. 27, 2026. The cause of the fire is under investigation. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
Felton said it’s not arson if someone sets a fire in an effort to stay warm. In that scenario, the proper charge might be trespassing. 
Rather, arson is often a crime of revenge, he said, with no statute of limitations. 
“That’s one thing about arson, it’s never what we believe,” Felton said. “It’s always what we can prove.” 

The day after the fire at the Village, black soot lined the top of the apartment building’s first floor. The windows were blown out, the blinds were melted, and the front door was boarded up with plywood, its metal sidings sitting misshapen on the front lawn. A pair of colorful kids’ sneakers sat on top of one of the sidings. 
Burned unit at the Village Apartments, located at 386 Raymond Road in south Jackson, Tuesday, Jan. 27, 2026. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
Around 3 p.m., a school bus wound through the complex, dropping dozens of kids off. Bundled up in puffer jackets, many proceeded to play, running around shipping containers containing construction materials for an ongoing renovation. Cruising down a sidewalk, two kids shared the same bicycle seat. The passenger excitedly explained to the driver his plans to acquire “two ice creams.” 
Multiple residents and an off-duty Jackson Police Department sergeant running security for the construction workers told Mississippi Today they had heard a rumor about the cause of the fire. But no one was certain of what, exactly, had transpired. 
That is, except for a 12-year-old girl in a hoodie, who immediately told this reporter that she had heard her friend who had moved into the complex last week started it by accident. She said he had been playing with a cigarette lighter when he dropped it on top of fuel leaking out of a dirt bike he had just taken inside.  
“I’m going to snitch,” she exclaimed. 
Felton said his investigator determined the fire originated in the apartment’s den, but its cause was still under investigation.
“We did hear that story, we just couldn’t substantiate that’s what happened,” Felton said. “But we heard all kinds of stories. Someone said the dirt bike ran through the fence. It was just a bunch of stories. We have to filter through what we heard and look for just the facts and things we can testify to in court.” 
A few buildings away, DeMetrius Bradley was helping his kids haul groceries inside. Holding a bottle of ketchup, he said it’s been cold in the apartments, despite the ongoing remodeling. 
“There’s a crack under the door,” he said. 
Bradley doesn’t stay at the complex, and he didn’t know the cause of the fire. But about three weeks ago, he said he helped save a family in another unit from a different fire around 1 a.m. 
Driving into the complex after a store-run, Bradley had seen flames leaping out of an upstairs window in a unit a few buildings away from the one that burned on Monday. 
“I’m glad I went to the store and got some cigarettes,” he said. 
He jumped out of his car and beat on the front door, waking up a woman who had fallen asleep on a couch. He said about four kids were crying for help in an upstairs bedroom. He helped them escape.
On Tuesday afternoon, soot could be seen around the upstairs window. Outside, a man was donning a hazmat suit. Renovators had discovered mold festering in the unit’s subflooring prior to the fire. The fungus outlived the fire, and he was there to kill it.

Unlike mold, fingerprints don’t survive flames. 
Oftentimes, the firefighters who respond to the scene can determine what caused the fire, and no further assessment is needed. 
But if the first responders suspect arson, Felton said his team will spring into action. His investigators also respond if a civilian or firefighter was injured or if a juvenile is believed to have started the fire. 
Once on scene, Felton explained that investigators start their evaluation at the unburned parts of the structure, working backward until they can identify the spot where the fire began. 
“It’s a tedious process,” he said. “That’s why a lot of those resources, those are more relegated to the fires that we feel are arson, other than someone is keeping warm.” 
“That fire was so big and hot it melted the side of this other building,” said Joe Jackson, speaking on the Saturday night fire that destroyed his Chapel Ridge apartment in south Jackson. Jackson stated he is now staying with a friend, Monday, Jan. 26, 2026 in Jackson. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
The goal is to classify the origin of the fire in one of four categories: Undetermined, natural, accidental or incendiary. Though people often confuse the latter category with arson, Felton said incendiary means only that a fire was started deliberately when it should not have been. 
“If someone used a barbecue grill in the kitchen that fire would be classified as incendiary because it was started by human involvement,” Felton said. “However it was not arson, it was stupidity.” 
Despite the investigators’ extensive training, proving whodunit can be tricky without eyewitnesses or security camera footage. That was the case with two fires this week: One on Terry Road on Tuesday that was caused by someone attempting to burn the insulation off copper wires, Felton said, resulting in embers jumping to the top of the building and igniting the roof. 
As firefighters “pulled up, they saw a trailer pulling away from the structure,” Felton said. No arrest has been made. 
The other was a fire that occurred Saturday evening at Chapel Ridge Apartments. 
The south Jackson apartment complex is supposed to be vacant after months of troubles that began when its owner, a 34-year-old New York real estate investor named Moshe “Mark” Silber, was imprisoned in connection with a fraud conspiracy last year. 
New owner Chapel Ridge Holdings LLC asked residents to move out by Jan. 1, according to a notice-to-vacate obtained by WLBT. But several residents remained.
The fire destroyed a four-unit building in the back of the complex near a ransacked dumpster, all but disintegrating its roof. Water that was used to fight the flames had frozen into a large, melting icicle, its droplets creating a pitter-patter sound. In front of the building, a suitcase of wet clothes was strewn on the grass. 
Khristina Miracle, left, chats with former neighbor Joe Jackson near the unit where they once lived at the Chapel Ridge Apartments located at 1636 Raymond Road in south Jackson, Monday, Jan. 26, 2026. The apartment unit was destroyed by a fire Saturday night. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
With no leads pointing to arson, such as the smell of gasoline, Felton said the department decided not to send in his investigators. Plus, he said firefighters have responded to eight fire-related incidents – be it a car or a unit on fire – at the complex since June. 
Two self-identified residents of Chapel Ridge Apartments on Raymond Road told Mississippi Today the fire was set intentionally.
The complex had been all but stripped of its parts. The metal coverings of the mailboxes by the front office had been ripped off. The front doors to nearly every unit hung open. 
Outside one of the unit’s Wednesday, a group of teenagers were playing music. One of them sat low to the ground on a gas-powered bike, wearing a bulky mask. Another had a swollen eye. They said they didn’t reside at the complex, nor did they know the cause of the fire. 
“We’re trying to make sure nothing like that happens again,” one said. 
Efforts to reach Larry Jackson, the senior asset manager for the complex’s new owners, were unsuccessful. Mississippi Today also could not reach Chapel Ridge Holdings. 
Though the notice from the new company said it would turn water and utilities off by Jan. 1, an Entergy spokesperson wrote in an email to Mississippi Today there are still “a few” active accounts at the complex. 
A woman named Khristina Miracle was there Tuesday, throwing her belongings off the balcony of the burned building. 
Khristina Miracle pitches what she can salvage of her burned belongings down to a shopping cart at the Chapel Ridge Apartments, located at 1636 Raymond Road in south Jackson, Monday, Jan. 26, 2026. Credit: Vickie D. King/Mississippi Today
The new owners had also told residents, through the notice to vacate, that the city could provide help with obtaining financial assistance for the move. Victoria Love, the city’s rental registration office manager, wrote in an email that 10 people called seeking such assistance. She did not know how many ultimately received help through a nonprofit. 
Months ago, Jackson’s Ward 5 Councilman Vernon Hartley had praised the company for stepping in to take over Chapel Ridge. When he visited a few months ago to provide the keys off to the new owner, Hartley said the complex was quiet. JPD had recently conducted a sweep. 
“It’s not a bad apartment, it’s not,” he said. 
After learning about the fire, Hartley said the city had done what it could to fix the problems. What happens now, he said, is “a business decision.”
But the councilman said he does want to find a solution to the number of homeless people in his ward. This week, he introduced a city ordinance to put a moratorium on new homeless services in Ward 5. He noted that a house down the street from his recently burned — the work, he suspected, of a homeless person. 
He said he didn’t know for sure if that was the case.

name: Rhode Island Current
title: RIPEC warns of unsustainable pace of state spending in FY27 budget analysis
description: State lawmakers, government watchdogs and community groups are now focused on the $14.9 billion total of Gov. Dan McKee’s proposed fiscal 2027 budget. But Michael DiBiase, president and CEO of the Rhode Island Public Expenditure Council (RIPEC), is more concerned about another number on the horizon: the $6.9 billion projected to come out of state […]
text:
A new report from the  Rhode Island Public Expenditure Council takes a long view of state spending, with a significant deficit projected just five years from now. (Photo by Alexander Castro/Rhode Island Current)State lawmakers, government watchdogs and community groups are now focused on the $14.9 billion total of Gov. Dan McKee’s proposed fiscal 2027 budget.
But Michael DiBiase, president and CEO of the Rhode Island Public Expenditure Council (RIPEC), is more concerned about another number on the horizon: the $6.9 billion projected to come out of state coffers in fiscal 2031. This represents a 16% increase over the $5.95 billion in state general revenue spending in McKee’s fiscal 2027 plan.
“It’s stunning,” DiBiase said.
Projected state revenue is expected to grow half as fast as state spending over the next five years. That’s why RIPEC called for caution in a new report published Thursday, a few weeks into the six-month legislative process under which lawmakers will reshape McKee’s proposal into a final budget. 
“If we don’t get control over this, I don’t know how we’re going to get from here to there,” said DiBiase.
It’s a familiar refrain by the independent business and tax policy group, which has been sounding alarms over state spending that exceeds revenue growth for years. State budget analysts, too, appeared worried over the projected deficit, which is expected to reach $537 million in five years. The projected fiscal 2027 deficit stands at $800,000.
But while McKee’s administrators blame federal budget cuts under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act or H.R. 1, for worsening Rhode Island’s financial future, DiBiase thinks the problem starts on Smith Hill.
For example, federal work requirements for Medicaid expected to kick 33,500 Rhode Islanders off the government insurance plan by the end of this year will cut costs for the state to administer the program. Rhode Island is expected to save $103.8 million in Medicaid costs, including $23.7 million of state funds, due to fewer enrollees in fiscal 2027, according to a separate state analysis.
Yet McKee’s spending proposal continues to grow the state share of Medicaid spending, adding $34.3 million over the current year’s budget. 
DiBiase called the continued growth in Medicaid spending, the single largest item in the state’s annual budget, “concerning.”
He acknowledged that federal policy changes will increase the number of residents without insurance, in turn raising costs for hospitals and state program administrators. But DiBiase refused to describe the federal overhaul as a budget “cut,” noting the dollars are increasing, just at a slower rate than under prior federal administrations.
Another concern: McKee’s proposed millionaire’s tax, which would add a 3% surtax on top of the 5.99% state income tax for filers who earn over $1 million a year. RIPEC joins other business groups like the Greater Providence Chamber of Commerce in opposing tax increases on top earners, warning of stifled economic growth and population declines.
“We already have a pretty progressive income tax compared with other states,” DiBiase said. “High earners carry a pretty great share of the burden.”
Increasing the state’s highest income tax bracket to 8.99% would make Rhode Island’s top income tax rate the eighth highest in the nation, up from the 15th highest currently, according to RIPEC.
Progressive Democratic lawmakers and policy advocates say McKee’s proposal does not go far enough in balancing the scales which have favored top earners for decades. A separate analysis by the Economic Progress Institute published on Wednesday estimates the state will bring in $600 million less in revenue this tax year as a result of ending a 8% flat tax on top earners two decades ago. The policy brief, incorporating data from the Institute on Taxation and Economic Policy, also notes the state’s top earners already save nearly $60,000 annually through federal income tax cuts made permanent under H.R. 1. 
Despite warnings of unsustainable spending patterns, RIPEC praised McKee’s near-term fiscal plan, which hinges on “relatively restrained” spending growth. The $5.95 billion in state spending included in McKee’s budget proposal is 2.5% more than the current year’s budget — the same as the expected revenue growth rate.
“I think the governor deserves praise in his effort to try to hold the line on state spending,” DiBiase said.
RIPEC noted the $600 million in bonds featured in McKee’s proposal was the highest in state history. But that doesn’t mean the policy group opposes the high level of borrowing at this time.
“Our debt level doesn’t compare well with other states but that’s not a major problem,” DiBiase said. “The question for policymakers over time is to decide which things we take on as new debt.”
